Educational content for Axioms of Probability is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Venn Diagrams is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Mutually Exclusive vs Independent is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Multiplication Rule is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Total Probability is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Bayes theorem describes the probability of an event based on prior knowledge of conditions that might be related to the event.
P(A|B) = \frac{P(B|A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}

metadata.P(A|B): The posterior probability: probability of A given that B is true.
metadata.P(B|A): The likelihood: probability of observing B given that A is true.
metadata.P(B): The marginal probability (total probability) of observing B.

blocks.content: It allows us to update our beliefs (probabilities) as new evidence arrives.
Educational content for Tree Diagrams is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Counting Principle is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Combinatorics is the study of counting. Permutations count arrangements where order matters, while combinations count selections where order does not matter.
P(n, r) = \frac{n!}{(n-r)!}

metadata.n!: Factorial of the total number of items.

blocks.content: C(n, r) = \binom{n}{r} = \frac{n!}{r!(n-r)!}

metadata.C(n, r): The number of combinations (order does not matter).
metadata.n!: Factorial of the total number of items.
metadata.r!: Factorial of the chosen number of items.

blocks.content: Think of permutations like a password (order matters), and combinations like a fruit salad (order doesn't matter)!
Educational content for Distinguishable Permutations is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
A random variable is a mathematical function that maps the outcomes of a random process to numerical values.
X: \Omega \to \mathbb{R}
We usually use uppercase letters (like X) for the random variable, and lowercase (x) for a specific value it can take!
Educational content for PMF & CDF is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
The Expected Value (or Mean) of a random variable is extremely well-behaved mathematically. It perfectly follows the rules of linearity.
E[aX + b] = aE[X] + b
E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y]

metadata.E[X + Y]: The expectation of the sum of two random variables

blocks.content: Linearity of expectation holds true EVEN IF X and Y are NOT independent!
Unlike the Expected Value, Variance is NOT linear. Adding a constant shifts the graph but doesn't change the spread, and multiplying by a constant scales the variance quadratically.
Var(aX + b) = a^2Var(X)

metadata.a^2: The constant squared, because variance measures squared deviations
metadata.b: The constant shift, which drops out because spread isn't affected by shifting

blocks.content: Var(X \pm Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) \pm 2Cov(X,Y)

metadata.Cov(X,Y): The covariance between X and Y. If they are independent, this is zero!

blocks.content: If X and Y are independent, Var(X - Y) is still Var(X) + Var(Y). Variances ALWAYS add up when things are independent, they never subtract!
Educational content for Bernoulli Trials is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
The Binomial distribution represents the number of successes in a sequence of n independent experiments.
P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}

metadata.\\binom{n}{k}: Combinations: Ways to choose k successes from n trials.
metadata.p^k: Probability of obtaining exactly k successes.
metadata.(1-p)^{n-k}: Probability of obtaining exactly n-k failures.

blocks.content: Remember that trials must be independent and the probability of success must be constant!
The Geometric distribution represents the number of trials needed to get the first success in repeated independent Bernoulli trials.

metadata.{p}: The probability of success on a single trial.
metadata.(1-p)^{k-1}: The probability of encountering exactly k-1 failures before the first success.

blocks.content: It is the only discrete memoryless random distribution!
The Poisson distribution expresses the probability of a given number of events occurring in a fixed interval of time or space.
P(X = k) = \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}

metadata.\\lambda^k: The average rate of success (λ) raised to the power of the target occurrences (k).
metadata.e^{-\\lambda}: Euler's number (e ≈ 2.718) raised to the negative average rate.
metadata.k!: The factorial of the target number of occurrences.

blocks.content: Use Poisson when events happen independently at a constant average rate!
Educational content for Hypergeometric Distribution is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Negative Binomial is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Continuous PDFs is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Integrals is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Continuous Expected Value is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
The Uniform distribution describes an experiment where there is an arbitrary outcome that lies between certain bounds. All intervals of the same length have equal probability.

metadata.{b}: The maximum possible value in the distribution's range.
metadata.{a}: The minimum possible value in the distribution's range.

blocks.content: Think of it as a completely fair random number generator between two limits.
The Normal distribution is a continuous probability distribution that is symmetrical around its mean, showing that data near the mean are more frequent.
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^2}

metadata.\\frac{1}{\\sigma \\sqrt{2\\pi}}: The normalization constant ensuring the total area under the curve is 1.
metadata.e^{-\\frac{1}{2}\\left(\\frac{x-\\mu}{\\: The exponential component that creates the symmetric bell curve shape.
metadata.\\mu: The mean (expected value) around which the distribution is centered.
metadata.\\sigma: The standard deviation, controlling the spread or width of the curve.

blocks.content: In a normal distribution, roughly 68% of the data falls within one standard deviation of the mean.
Educational content for Standard Normal is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
The Exponential distribution describes the time between events in a Poisson point process (e.g., waiting times).
f(x) = {\lambda} e^{-\lambda x}

metadata.{\\lambda}: The rate parameter (events per unit of time/space).
metadata.e^{-\\lambda x}: The exponential decay representing the probability of no event occurring until time x.

blocks.content: It is the continuous analogue of the geometric distribution, and it is also memoryless.
Educational content for Gamma Distribution is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Beta Distribution is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Joint Distributions is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Marginal Distributions is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Conditional Distributions is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Covariance measures the directional relationship between two random variables. A positive covariance means they tend to move together; negative means they move inversely.
Cov(X,Y) = E[(X - E[X])(Y - E[Y])]
Covariance gives direction, but not strength. For strength, you need to normalize it into Correlation!
The Pearson correlation coefficient is the covariance of the two variables divided by the product of their standard deviations. It always falls between -1 and 1.
\rho_{X,Y} = \frac{Cov(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}
Correlation does not imply causation! It only measures linear relationships.
Educational content for Independence of RVs is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Linear Combinations is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
The mean represents the mathematical average of a set of numbers.
\mu = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}{N}

metadata.\\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i: The sum of all values in the population.
metadata.N: The total number of items in the population.

blocks.content: \bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}

metadata.\\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i: The sum of all values in the sample.
metadata.n: The total number of items in the sample.

blocks.content: The population mean divides by N, while the sample mean divides by n.
The median is the middle value when a dataset is ordered, making it robust against outliers. The mode is the most frequently occurring value.
Median = \left( \frac{n + 1}{2} \right)^{th} \text{term}

metadata.n: Total number of items in the ordered dataset

blocks.content: Use the median instead of the mean when your data is highly skewed (like income brackets)!
Educational content for Weighted Mean is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Range and IQR is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Variance measures how far a set of numbers is spread out from their average value. Standard deviation is its square root.
\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^2}{N}

metadata.(x_i - \\mu)^2: The squared difference between each value and the population mean.
metadata.N: The total number of items in the population.

metadata.(x_i - \\bar{x})^2: The squared difference between each value and the sample mean.
metadata.n - 1: Bessel's correction (degrees of freedom) for unbiased estimation.

blocks.content: \sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}

metadata.\\sigma: The standard deviation, which is the square root of the variance.

blocks.content: Sample variance uses (n-1) to correct for bias when estimating the population variance!
Educational content for Standard Deviation is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Coefficient of Variation is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Percentiles is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Educational content for Z-Scores is coming soon. Stay tuned for future app updates!
Skewness is a measure of the asymmetry of the probability distribution. A negative skew means the tail is on the left; a positive skew means the tail is on the right.
Skewness = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^3}{(n-1)s^3}

metadata.\\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \\bar{x})^3: Sum of cubed differences from the mean (third moment)
metadata.s^3: Cube of the sample standard deviation

blocks.content: If mean > median, the data is usually positively skewed (skewed right).

An extension of simple linear regression used to predict an outcome variable based on multiple distinct predictor variables.
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots + \beta_n X_n + \epsilon
Watch out for multicollinearity—if your predictor variables are highly correlated with each other, it ruins your model's stability.

Despite its name, logistic regression is a classification algorithm. It uses the sigmoid function to map predicted values to probabilities between 0 and 1, usually to predict a binary outcome (Yes/No, True/False).

metadata.e^{-(\\beta_0 + \\beta_1 X)}: The exponential of the negative linear regression equation

blocks.content: This is essentially the foundational math behind the neurons in a neural network!
